topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The ballroom was a dizzying swirl of satin and forced laughter. I clutched my champagne flute so tightly my knuckles were white, trying to appear engaged in a conversation about the canapés, but my eyes kept drifting toward the entrance. Then, there he was. Liam. He was standing near the punch bowl, laughing with someone I vaguely recognized, and the sound of his voice, warm and familiar, hit me like a physical blow. My breath hitched, a shallow, panicked intake of air. Every muscle in my jaw tightened. I forced a smile, a brittle thing that felt alien on my face, and steered myself toward the periphery, needing distance, needing to dissolve into the wallpaper. When his gaze swept across the room and snagged on mine, the easy light in his eyes flickered, and for a split second, the entire polished room seemed to tilt off its axis. I mumbled an excuse and fled toward the terrace doors, the cool night air a welcome, sharp relief against the sudden, internal heat radiating from my chest.

[story 2]

I was supposed to be celebrating Sarah’s happiness, but the music felt muffled, distant. I kept replaying every shared glance, every unspoken word from the past few weeks, and the memory of Liam’s easy smile was a jagged shard in my throat. Then, across the crowded dance floor, I saw him. He was leaning against a pillar, looking impossibly composed in a dark suit. A strange, cold numbness spread through my limbs, making it hard to move. My feet felt rooted to the marble floor. I watched him, cataloging every subtle shift in his posture, searching for any sign that he might acknowledge my presence. When our eyes met—a brief, unavoidable collision—a wave of profound emptiness washed over me. I quickly turned away, my practiced composure failing; my hand instinctively flew up to grip my own arm, a desperate anchor against the sudden, alarming drift toward the edge.

[story 3]

The air in the reception hall was thick with perfume and obligation. I was nursing a glass of sparkling cider, trying to appear perfectly content, a performance I was utterly failing. It was then I saw him near the bar, looking relaxed, utterly unburdened. A sudden, sharp constriction seized my chest, stealing the oxygen. My vision narrowed, focusing solely on him. It was him. The years had smoothed the edges of the memory, but seeing him now, standing so effortlessly in a space that once felt exclusively ours, sent a tremor through me. I felt a strange, heavy weight settle in my stomach, like stones. I deliberately lowered my gaze, focusing intently on a distant chandelier, trying to quell the involuntary tremor running through my hands. I needed to disappear, to become invisible, before the reality of that shared history could overwhelm me in front of everyone.
